title: Alex Petrov — Security Advisor
compiled_truth:
Alex Petrov is a security advisor to [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor), where he focuses on post-quantum migration strategy and regulatory compliance framing for the company's deterministic data infrastructure. He joined the advisory board in early 2026, shortly after Anchor's seed round closed. His background is deeply rooted in applied cryptography — he spent nearly a decade at the NSA working on classified signal-processing and authentication systems before transitioning to the private sector, where he consulted for defense contractors and financial institutions on cryptographic hardening.

Petrov is known for being analytical to a fault. In meetings he'll sit quietly for twenty minutes, then dismantle an entire architectural assumption with a single question. Several team members have described his style as "uncomfortably precise," which is exactly why [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) brought him on. His technical depth in lattice-based schemes and hash-based signature constructions has been instrumental in shaping how Anchor thinks about longevity — specifically, ensuring that the single-pass primitives at the core of the product don't become liabilities when quantum-capable adversaries arrive.

Alex doesn't have an equity stake in the traditional sense; his compensation is structured around milestone-based advisory grants, which he apparently negotiated himself. He's not interested in board governance or fundraising theatrics. He wants to work on hard problems, and Anchor gives him a sandbox to do that.

One area where Petrov has had outsized impact is compliance. He helped draft Anchor's internal "crypto-agility" framework — a set of guidelines for swapping underlying primitves without breaking determinism guarantees. This framework became the basis for Anchor's SOC 2 narrative around cryptographic controls, and Petrov personally walked the auditors through the threat model during the Type II examination in mid-2027.

Outside of Anchor, he occasionally publishes on post-quantum transition risks in infrastructure software, though he avoids conferences and rarely does press. He's based in the D.C. metro area and typically flies out to Anchor's offices once a quarter. Most of his advisory work happens asynchronously — long, detailed memos that the engineering team treats almost like internal RFCs. He's not flashy, but his fingerprints are on some of the most critical design decisions Anchor has made.
timeline:
- **2026-02-10** | Alex Petrov formally joins [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) as Security Advisor following introduction through a mutual contact in the defense sector.
- **2026-04-18** | Delivers first internal memo on post-quantum migration risk, outlining a phased approach to crypto-agility within Anchor's single-pass architecture.
- **2026-08-03** | Participates in a two-day on-site architecture review at Anchor's office; raises concerns about key derivation assumptions that lead to a sprint-level redesign.
- **2026-11-22** | Publishes a short paper on post-quantum transition timelines for deterministic systems, which circulates widely in infrastructure security circles.
- **2027-03-14** | Helps [Anchor Codec](companies/anchor) draft the crypto-agility framework that becomes central to their compliance positioning.
- **2027-06-29** | Walks SOC 2 Type II auditors through Anchor's cryptographic threat model during the on-site portion of the examination.
- **2027-09-11** | Advises on Anchor's response to a customer RFP requiring FIPS 140-level assurances; helps shape a compliance roadmap that avoids premature certification costs.
- **2028-01-20** | Quarterly on-site visit; reviews Anchor's updated authentication pipeline and signs off on the post-quantum readiness milestones for the year.
- **2028-05-08** | Petrov's advisory grant hits its second milestone trigger following Anchor's Series A close; renews advisory agreement for another 18 months.

_facts.name: Alex Petrov
_facts.title: Security Advisor
_facts.background: Former NSA and private sector applied cryptography; advises on post-quantum migration and compliance framing
